question: What is a test case? What makes a good one?
answer:
A test case is a documented set of steps, data, and expected results used to verify a specific behaviour.

A good test case is clear, reusable, and independent. It usually includes: ID, title, preconditions, steps, test data, expected result, and sometimes priority/severity.
tip: Another tester should be able to execute it without asking you what you meant.
deep:
Minimal template

FieldExample
TitleLogin with valid credentials
PreconditionsUser exists; app is on login page
Steps1) Enter email 2) Enter password 3) Click Login
Test datauser@test.com / Valid@123
ExpectedUser lands on Home; session cookie set

What "good" looks like

  • One main behaviour per case (don’t mix login + checkout).
  • Observable expected result (UI message, DB row, API status).
  • Traceable to a requirement/story ID when possible.

How to say it in an interview

“A test case is a repeatable recipe: preconditions, steps, data, and expected result. I write it so anyone on the team can run it and know pass/fail without guessing.”

question: Explain the defect / bug life cycle.
answer:
Typical flow: New → Assigned → Open/In Progress → Fixed → Retest → Verified/Closed. Branches: Rejected (not a bug), Deferred (fix later), Reopened (fix failed), Duplicate.

Tools like Jira track status, owner, and history so triage stays organised.
tip: Always retest after fix — and check nearby areas for regression.
deep:
Statuses in plain language

  • New: logged, not yet reviewed.
  • Assigned / Open: developer owns it.
  • Fixed / Ready for QA: developer believes it is done.
  • Retest: QA verifies the fix.
  • Closed: confirmed fixed (or accepted as not a defect).
  • Reopened: fix did not work or bug returned.

What a good bug report needs

  • Clear title, environment, build/version.
  • Steps to reproduce, actual vs expected.
  • Severity/priority, screenshots/logs, evidence.

How to say it in an interview

“I log defects with clear repro steps, triage severity/priority, track them through fix and retest, and reopen if the issue persists. The lifecycle exists so nothing is lost between QA, dev, and release.”

question: Severity vs Priority — what’s the difference?
answer:
Severity = how badly the defect impacts the system (technical impact). Priority = how soon it should be fixed (business urgency).

Example: a typo on the homepage can be low severity but high priority before a marketing launch. A crash in a rarely used admin screen can be high severity but low priority.
tip: QA usually proposes severity; product/lead often sets priority.
deep:
Common severity scale

  • Critical/Blocker: system down, data loss, no workaround.
  • Major: major feature broken, workaround may exist.
  • Minor: limited impact, UI/functional nuisance.
  • Trivial: cosmetic.

Interview trap

People treat them as the same. Show you can give a mismatched example — that proves you understand triage, not just definitions.

How to say it in an interview

“Severity is impact on the product; priority is order of fixing for the business. I classify severity from evidence, then align priority with stakeholders during defect triage.”

question: Smoke vs Sanity vs Regression testing?
answer:
• Smoke — shallow, wide check that the build is testable (app launches, main pages open). Fail smoke → reject build.
  • Sanity — narrow, deeper check of a specific fix/area after a small change.
  • Regression — broader suite to ensure old features still work after changes.
tip: Smoke = build stability; Sanity = fix confidence; Regression = no side effects.
deep:
When you run each

TypeWhenScope
SmokeNew build arrivesCritical paths only, quick
SanityAfter a bug fix / small patchAffected feature
RegressionBefore release / after larger changesExisting features

SDET angle

Smoke and regression are prime automation candidates in CI. Sanity is often a short manual or targeted automated pack around the ticket.

How to say it in an interview

“Smoke decides if the build is worth testing. Sanity checks the specific change. Regression protects existing behaviour. I automate smoke/regression where possible and keep sanity focused on the ticket.”

question: What is UAT (User Acceptance Testing)? How is it different from system testing?
answer:
UAT is acceptance testing by business users/clients to confirm the system meets real-world needs and is ready for production.

System testing is usually done by QA against requirements in a test environment. UAT focuses on business workflows, real data scenarios, and sign-off — not every low-level validation.
tip: UAT success = business acceptance, not just 'QA passed'.
deep:
What SDETs/QA do in UAT

  • Prepare UAT scenarios and data with stakeholders.
  • Support users, capture defects, clarify expected business behaviour.
  • Track sign-off criteria and open risks.

Difference that matters

QA may verify "order status updates to Shipped". UAT users ask "Can our operations team process Friday’s peak orders the way they work today?" — process fit, not only functional correctness.

How to say it in an interview

“System testing validates requirements from a QA lens. UAT validates business readiness with real users. I’ve led UAT by preparing scenarios, guiding stakeholders, and driving defect triage to sign-off.”

question: What are Equivalence Partitioning and Boundary Value Analysis?
answer:
Equivalence Partitioning (EP) groups inputs that should behave the same, then tests one value from each group (valid/invalid partitions).

Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) tests the edges of those groups — bugs often live at min/max limits (e.g. 0, 1, 100, 101 for a 1–100 range).
tip: EP reduces cases; BVA catches off-by-one defects.
deep:
Example: age field allowed 18–60

  • EP valid: 18–60 → pick 30.
  • EP invalid: <18, >60, non-numeric → pick 17, 61, "abc".
  • BVA: 17, 18, 19 and 59, 60, 61.

Why interviewers ask

It shows you design tests systematically instead of randomly clicking. Mention combining EP + BVA for efficient coverage.

How to say it in an interview

“I use equivalence partitioning to avoid redundant cases, then boundary value analysis on the edges because off-by-one defects are common. Together they give strong coverage with fewer tests.”

question: What should a good bug report contain?
answer: A good bug report lets someone reproduce and fix without guessing: clear title, environment/build, preconditions, steps, test data, actual vs expected result, severity/priority, evidence (screenshots/logs/API response), and optionally related story/module.
tip: If the developer can’t reproduce from your report, the report failed.
code:
Title: Checkout total ignores 10% coupon CODE10 on QA
Env: QA v24.6.1, Chrome 126
Steps: ...
Actual: Total remains 100
Expected: Total becomes 90
Evidence: screenshot + network response
deep:
Weak vs strong title

  • Weak: "Checkout bug"
  • Strong: "Coupon CODE10 not applied to order total on QA Chrome"

How to say it in an interview

“I write defects for reproducibility — exact build, steps, data, actual vs expected, and evidence. That speeds triage and fix verification.”

question: Test scenario vs test case?
answer: A test scenario is a high-level what-to-test statement ("Verify user can reset password"). A test case is the detailed how — steps, data, expected results for that scenario.
tip: One scenario often expands into multiple cases (valid email, expired token, etc.).
code:
Scenario: User resets password
Cases: valid reset, expired link, mismatched confirm password
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“Scenarios define coverage themes. Cases make them executable and measurable with clear expected results.”

question: What is decision table testing? Give an example.
answer: Decision tables map combinations of conditions to actions/outcomes. Useful when business rules have multiple inputs (role + order status + payment state → allowed actions).
tip: Great for complex business rules in telecom/BSS/order systems.
code:
VIP=Y, Order=OPEN, Paid=Y → Allow cancel with refund
VIP=N, Order=SHIPPED, Paid=Y → Cancel blocked
deep:
Beginner walkthrough

List conditions as columns, each row a combination, and expected system action. Then create one test case per important row (or pairwise if combinations explode).

How to say it in an interview

“When rules depend on multiple conditions, I use a decision table so we don’t miss an allowed/denied combination — common in order management and entitlement flows.”

question: What is state transition testing?
answer: State transition testing validates that an entity moves between allowed states with valid events, and rejects invalid transitions. Example order states: CREATED → PAID → SHIPPED → DELIVERED; cannot jump CREATED → DELIVERED.
tip: Draw the state diagram, then test valid paths and illegal transitions.
deep:
What to test

  • Happy path transitions.
  • Invalid transition attempts (API/UI).
  • Terminal states (CANCELLED/DELIVERED) don’t accept further illegal updates.

How to say it in an interview

“For order lifecycle I model states and events, then verify both allowed transitions and that illegal ones are blocked with clear errors.”

question: How do you derive tests from a use case / user story?
answer: From the main success path, alternate paths, and exceptions. Convert acceptance criteria into scenarios, then into cases with data. Include negative and boundary conditions the story implies.
tip: Ask: happy path, failure path, and authorization path for every story.
code:
Story: Cancel order within 1 hour
- Success within 60 min
- Reject after 61 min
- Unauthorized user cannot cancel
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“I turn acceptance criteria into positive, negative, and permission cases, then add data/DB checks for side effects like refunds.”

question: How do you design and manage test data?
answer: Identify data needed per scenario (users, orders, products), keep it reusable and unique where collisions matter, document setup/cleanup, and avoid depending on fragile production-like leftovers. Prefer dedicated QA accounts and factories/scripts for repeatable data.
tip: Flaky manual and automated tests often mean shared mutable data.
code:
qa.order.user+<timestamp>@test.com
seed order in CREATED status for cancel tests
deep:
Good practices

  • Unique emails/IDs per run.
  • Known fixtures for each state you need.
  • Cleanup or isolated tenants.
  • Never use real customer PII in shared docs.

How to say it in an interview

“I design data for independence — each scenario gets known preconditions. That makes defects reproducible and automation stable.”

question: What is a blocker defect? How do you handle it in a release?
answer: A blocker prevents major testing or a critical user journey (can’t login, can’t place order). You escalate immediately, pause dependent testing if needed, and treat it as release-stopping unless a formal waiver exists.
tip: Communicate impact clearly: who is blocked and which exit criteria fail.
deep:
Handling steps

  1. Confirm reproducibility + severity.
  2. Escalate in triage with business impact.
  3. Retest ASAP after fix.
  4. Run focused regression around the area.

How to say it in an interview

“Blockers stop critical flows or testing progress. I escalate with evidence, track fix urgently, and retest plus nearby regression before continuing release sign-off.”

question: What is ad-hoc testing? How is it different from exploratory testing?
answer: Ad-hoc is informal, unstructured testing without documented design. Exploratory is also unscripted but more disciplined — time-boxed charters, notes, and follow-up cases. Exploratory is preferred professionally.
tip: Don’t rely on pure ad-hoc for release evidence.
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“Ad-hoc is freeform clicking. Exploratory is structured investigation with a mission and notes I can convert into reusable tests.”

question: Alpha testing vs Beta testing?
answer: Alpha is performed in-house (internal users/QA) before external release. Beta is performed by limited external real users in a production-like setting to gather feedback before full launch.
tip: Alpha = internal; Beta = external real users.
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“Alpha validates readiness internally. Beta validates real-world usage and feedback before broad release.”

question: How do you track test execution status?
answer: Track each case as Pass/Fail/Blocked/Not Run (and sometimes Skipped). Summarise by module/priority, link failures to defects, and report progress against exit criteria daily during release testing.
tip: Blocked ≠ Failed — blocked means can’t run due to dependency/environment.
code: High priority: 40 Pass / 2 Fail / 1 Blocked / 7 Not Run
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“I report execution with clear statuses and defect links so stakeholders see risk, not just a percentage complete.”

question: What happens in a defect triage meeting?
answer: Triage reviews new/open defects with QA, dev, and often product: confirm validity, set/adjust severity & priority, assign owners, target fix version, and decide defer/reject/duplicate. Goal is a clear, prioritised backlog.
tip: Come prepared with repro evidence and business impact.
deep:
Your role as SDET/QA

  • Explain impact with evidence.
  • Suggest severity from technical impact.
  • Accept priority from business when appropriate.
  • Call out release risks if blockers remain.

How to say it in an interview

“In triage I present reproducible defects with impact, help classify severity/priority, and ensure critical issues get owners and fix targets before release.”

question: What is error guessing?
answer: Error guessing uses experience to invent likely failure cases that formal techniques may miss — double-submit, empty optional-but-used fields, copy-paste whitespace, timezone boundaries, concurrent edits.
tip: Pair error guessing with EP/BVA — experience fills gaps in models.
code:
Double-click Place Order → two orders?
Paste email with trailing space → login fails?
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“I use error guessing based on past defects — especially concurrency, bad input formatting, and partial failures in multi-step order flows.”

question: What is build acceptance / BVT testing?
answer: Build Verification Testing (BVT) / build acceptance is a short smoke pack that decides if a new build is stable enough for deeper testing. If BVT fails, the build is rejected.
tip: BVT should be fast, automated when possible, and cover only critical paths.
code: BVT: login, open dashboard, create order draft, logout
deep:
How to say it in an interview

“I run a small BVT/smoke first. If core journeys fail, I reject the build instead of wasting a day on full regression.”

question: What is included in a test closure / summary report?
answer: A closure report summarises scope tested, execution results, open defects (by severity), risks/waivers, environment/build, and recommendation (go/no-go). It is the evidence behind sign-off.
tip: Leaders need risk clarity more than raw case counts.
deep:
Useful sections

  • What was in scope / out of scope.
  • Pass-fail summary by priority.
  • Open Sev-1/2 list with impact.
  • Known limitations and residual risk.
  • Sign-off recommendation.

How to say it in an interview

“At closure I report coverage, open risks, and a clear go/no-go recommendation tied to exit criteria — not just ‘testing completed’.”
